relationship_summary: China is Iran's primary economic lifeline and the single most important external actor sustaining the Iranian state's fiscal viability. China absorbs approximately 90% of Iran's oil exports, provides diplomatic cover at the UN Security Council, backed Iran's accession to the SCO (2023) and BRICS (2024), and signed a 25-year Comprehensive Strategic Partnership in March 2021 nominally worth up to $400 billion in investment. However, the relationship is fundamentally asymmetric. China's total two-way trade with Saudi Arabia and the UAE each reached approximately $108 billion in 2025, dwarfing the $41.2 billion (including unreported oil) with Iran. China's exports to Iran dropped 22% in 2025 to below $7 billion, and non-oil imports from Iran fell 32% to just $3 billion — meaning that outside of discounted oil purchases, the commercial relationship is thin and shrinking. More critically, China has far more to lose from a direct US confrontation than it gains from defending Iran. China's economic interdependence with the United States and its prioritization of the Taiwan issue mean that Beijing will consistently calibrate its Iran support to avoid crossing thresholds that would provoke serious US retaliation. This creates a relationship where Iran has extreme dependency but limited leverage: Iran cannot credibly threaten to cut off China (it has no alternative buyer at scale), while China can modulate purchases, delay investment, and distance itself diplomatically whenever Iranian actions risk entangling Beijing in unwanted confrontation. During the 2026 conflict, China condemned US-Israeli strikes, dispatched diplomatic envoys, continued purchasing oil via shadow fleet, and blocked UNSC enforcement actions — but simultaneously pressured Iran on the Strait of Hormuz closure to protect Qatari gas exports vital to Chinese energy security, declined to provide large-scale military resupply, and de-escalated around Taiwan to preserve the planned Trump-Xi summit. China is 'playing for the long term' — benefiting passively from US overextension while avoiding any action that would make it a co-belligerent.

economic_ties.oil_trade: China imported approximately 1.38 million barrels per day (bpd) of Iranian oil in 2025, representing about 12–13.4% of China's total crude imports and absorbing 80–90% of Iran's total oil exports (confirmed by multiple tracking sources including Kpler, UANI, and The Diplomat). The true value of Iranian crude exports to China was approximately $31.2 billion in 2025, comprising over 75% of total bilateral trade. Oil revenue from China accounts for roughly 35–45% of Iran's government budget, making it the single most important revenue stream sustaining the Iranian state. Iranian crude trades at a consistent $8–10/barrel discount to Brent (some sources cite $3–9, with occasional reports of discounts as steep as $6 below already-discounted levels). Under the leaked terms of the 25-year agreement, China secured a guaranteed minimum discount of 12% to the six-month rolling mean of comparable benchmarks, plus an additional 6–8% risk-adjusted compensation. Nearly all Iranian oil exports to China are settled in yuan/renminbi, typically through small Chinese banks with minimal international exposure (the Bank of Kunlun was a key conduit until renewed US pressure in 2018). This arrangement is significant for both parties: Iran receives hard currency outside the dollar system, while China secures below-market energy supplies and advances its broader de-dollarization objectives. Pre-war stockpiling was aggressive — Iran exported 2.16 million bpd in February 2026 (highest since July 2018), virtually all to China. Volumes fluctuated: Kpler data showed imports plummeted from about 1.6 million bpd in September 2024 to 740,000 bpd by April 2025 under stepped-up US enforcement, before recovering to 1.38 million bpd average for the full year. During the 2026 conflict, China continues receiving approximately 1.25 million bpd, with Chinese-flagged vessels granted passage through the Iranian-controlled Strait while others are blocked. NOTE: Chinese customs authorities officially report zero oil imports from Iran; volumes are falsely attributed to Malaysia, Oman, Indonesia, and the UAE.
economic_ties.trade_volume: Total bilateral trade including unreported oil was estimated at approximately $41.2 billion in 2025. However, official (non-oil) trade figures tell a starkly different story about the relationship's depth. Chinese exports to Iran dropped 22% in 2025 to below $7 billion, while Chinese imports from Iran (excluding oil) declined 32% to just $3 billion — indicating the non-oil commercial relationship is thin and deteriorating. For comparison, China-Israel bilateral trade reached approximately $33 billion in 2025 with 48% growth, and China's trade with Saudi Arabia and the UAE each exceeded $108 billion. BRI investment in Iran has been negligible despite the 25-year agreement's nominal $400 billion commitment. A February 2026 Green Finance & Development Center report found that while Chinese BRI investment surged 62% globally to over $85 billion in 2025, Iran (like Russia) received almost none of it — Kazakhstan alone received $25.8 billion, thirty-eight times more than Russia's $674 million, with Iran receiving even less. Chinese companies remain reluctant to risk sanctions exposure. A Wall Street Journal investigation in October 2025 reported that Sinosure, a Chinese state-owned enterprise, may have secretly facilitated up to $8.4 billion in investment via a 'construction-for-oil' exchange, but this is disputed and unconfirmed at scale. Iran's key port infrastructure includes Chabahar and Bandar Abbas; China opened its first consulate in Iran at Bandar Abbas to support Gulf coast investment, and the 25-year agreement envisions development of Iran's Makran coast. China demonstrated the viability of the Qom-Yiwu-Europe rail route in 2024. However, Iran has been largely sidelined from major BRI corridor investments, with transit volume limited to 22 million tons annually, and Chabahar port handling less than 2% of Iran's foreign trade volume. The gap between the 25-year agreement's aspirational framework and actual implementation remains enormous.
economic_ties.sanctions_evasion: China has built what analysts describe as a 'sanctions-evasion economy' to sustain Iranian oil flows. The mechanism operates through several layers. First, the shadow fleet: Iran's oil reaches China through a fleet of aging tankers (the 'ghost fleet' or 'dark fleet') that sail under foreign flags, disable AIS transponders, spoof GPS locations, and conduct ship-to-ship (STS) transfers at sea — primarily in Malaysian waters, approximately 20 miles offshore. UANI recorded 679 STS transfer instances in Malaysian waters in 2024, up from 280 in 2023. In 2025, shadow tankers made over 1,500 trips from Iran to China. Second, the refinery network: since major Chinese state-owned refineries stepped back from Iranian crude after 2018, purchases shifted to 'teapot' refineries — small, nominally independent refineries clustered in Shandong province that were only granted import licenses in 2015. By 2025, teapots handled an estimated 90% of Iran's oil exports to China, providing Beijing 'plausible deniability.' However, Kharon research shows these teapots maintain close ties to state-owned enterprises through joint ventures and partnerships. Third, financial intermediaries: payments are settled in renminbi through small Chinese banks with minimal international exposure, Hong Kong-based front companies, and shell entities registered in the UAE, Singapore, Oman, Iraq, and Malaysia that help falsify documentation and rebrand Iranian crude. Chinese customs data attributes Iranian oil to Malaysia, Indonesia, and other origins — China officially reported zero oil imports from Iran in 2024, yet imported over 1.4 million bpd from 'Malaysia,' triple that country's total production capacity. Fourth, dual-use technology: China has exported radar systems, navigation technology, and electronic warfare tools to Iran. The confirmed provision of full military access to China's BeiDou-3 encrypted satellite navigation system in 2021 is the most significant confirmed technology transfer, and experts believe Iran is using it to guide attacks in the 2026 conflict. US enforcement has targeted this network — the February 2026 OFAC round sanctioned another dozen vessels, and in 2025 Treasury sanctioned Hebei Xinhai Chemical Group and the Zarringhalam family network — but the system has grown more efficient, with voyage times shortened from 85–90 days to 50–70 days.

diplomatic_support: China provides Iran with multi-layered diplomatic support that falls short of alliance but exceeds normal partnership. At the UN Security Council, China (alongside Russia) has consistently blocked or diluted US-led efforts to impose additional sanctions and enforcement measures. In March 2026, China and Russia blocked the US-drafted Security Council programme of work because it included a briefing on the 1737 Sanctions Committee, whose authority both countries refuse to recognize — rendering multilateral enforcement 'functionally inoperable.' China backed Iran's full SCO membership (approved 2021, effective 2023) after 15 years of Iranian applications, and supported Iran's accession to BRICS in 2024. At the September 2025 SCO summit in Tianjin, Presidents Xi and Pezeshkian pledged to elevate ties to their 'highest level,' with Iran framing SCO as a platform for Global South interests and de-dollarization. China's official position on the 2026 conflict condemns US-Israeli strikes as lacking UN authorization and violating international law; Foreign Minister Wang Yi called the killing of Khamenei 'unacceptable.' China dispatched Special Envoy Zhai Jun on a regional tour starting March 8. However, China's support has clear limits on what it will say and do publicly. BRICS issued a statement expressing 'concern' about the strikes but took no position — reflecting the bloc's limited cohesion. China refused Trump's demand to police the Strait of Hormuz but simultaneously pressured Iran to protect Qatari LNG exports. China's public framing emphasizes restraint, peaceful resolution, and mediation rather than endorsement of Iranian military actions. ISW/AEI assessed China is 'unlikely to provide material support to Iran' — and the overall pattern suggests China positions itself as a diplomatic counterweight to Western pressure rather than a military partner.

chinese_red_lines.description: China will not provide direct military involvement in any Iran conflict, will not extend formal mutual defense commitments, and will calibrate all support to avoid provoking serious US retaliation. The 2026 conflict has made these limits visible. While Middle East Eye reported (February 27, citing three officials) that China delivered 'offensive' weapons including loitering munitions and air defense systems before the strikes, and Reuters reported Iran was nearing a deal for CM-302 supersonic anti-ship missiles, China's MFA denied the CM-302 report, and ISW/AEI assessed large-scale wartime resupply as unlikely. The most credible picture shows pre-war transfers of limited systems, ongoing negotiations, but no confirmed large-scale wartime support. Two Iranian ships departed China carrying sodium perchlorate (a solid rocket fuel precursor) the week of March 2, but this falls far short of the intelligence-sharing and military equipment Russia is providing. US intelligence describes China as 'more cautious' than Russia — it 'may be preparing' financial assistance and spare parts but wants the war to end because it endangers Chinese energy supplies. China will reduce oil purchases and distance itself if Iranian actions directly threaten Chinese economic interests: the Strait of Hormuz closure threatens Qatari LNG exports critical to Chinese energy security, and China is actively pressuring Iran on this point. The threshold appears to be: China will sustain the economic relationship and provide diplomatic cover as long as the costs of doing so remain below the costs of a serious deterioration in US-China relations or disruption to China's broader energy security architecture.
chinese_red_lines.us_relationship_constraint: China's economic interdependence with the United States is the primary structural constraint on its Iran support. US-China bilateral trade dwarfs China-Iran trade by orders of magnitude. Any Chinese action that provokes serious US secondary sanctions on major Chinese financial institutions or state-owned enterprises would impose costs far exceeding the benefits of the Iran relationship. The Taiwan priority further constrains China: during the 2026 conflict, Taiwan recorded 13 consecutive days without Chinese warplane approaches — described as 'unlike anything we've seen in recent history' — and PLA ADIZ incursions dropped below 200/month from 300+/month averages. Analysts attribute this to China's desire to preserve the planned Trump-Xi summit and avoid giving the US a pretext for redirecting military attention to the Indo-Pacific. China is benefiting passively from US overextension (the removal of THAAD radars from South Korea is an intelligence windfall, US carrier groups and Patriot batteries have been pulled from the Indo-Pacific), but it has chosen strategic patience over opportunistic aggression. China's 2026 defense spending increased 7% to approximately $278 billion, and its government work report strengthened language on Taiwan from 'oppose' to 'crack down on' independence — but these are long-term preparations, not crisis exploitation. The calculus is clear: the Taiwan contingency requires a favorable US-China diplomatic baseline and undivided PLA preparation. Entanglement in an Iran conflict would jeopardize both. As one framework puts it, China has 'far more to lose from a direct US confrontation than it has to gain from defending Iran.'

yuan_oil_mechanism: The yuan-for-oil arrangement between Iran and China operates through a layered system of small Chinese banks, renminbi settlement accounts, and intermediary shell companies that keep transactions outside the dollar-based SWIFT system. Nearly all Iranian oil exports to China are settled in yuan. The Bank of Kunlun was historically the primary conduit until renewed US pressure in 2018; since then, payments have dispersed across multiple small banks with minimal international exposure. This bilateral mechanism is part of a broader Chinese de-dollarization infrastructure: the Shanghai International Energy Exchange launched yuan-denominated crude futures in March 2018 (accounting for approximately 10.5% of global crude futures volume by 2020); Russia-China crude oil trade settled $19.14 billion in yuan in 2025; and the mBridge digital currency platform processed over $55.5 billion in transactions by November 2025, with digital yuan accounting for 95%. However, the yuan's share of global oil payments remained below 5% as of mid-2025, confined primarily to bilateral deals with Russia, Iran, and Venezuela. The US dollar still facilitates approximately 80–90% of global oil transactions, and the yuan comprises only about 2% of global reserves versus the dollar's 57%. The Iran-China oil arrangement is strategically significant not because it threatens immediate dollar displacement but because it demonstrates a functioning alternative payment architecture for sanctioned states — a proof of concept that could expand if more countries face Western financial exclusion. The Hormuz dimension adds symbolic weight: Iran is 'considering' allowing tanker passage for yuan-denominated cargo (CNN, March 14, 2026), though this remains a proposal under consideration, not operational policy. Chinese analysts reacted cautiously, citing feasibility limits and potential strain on China-US relations. The Gulf Research Center chairman called the immediate practical impact 'limited' but symbolically significant. The dollar's share of global reserves has declined from 72% in 2001 to approximately 57% in 2025, and economists estimate the petrodollar 'exorbitant privilege' is worth roughly $225–270 billion annually to the US economy — so even marginal acceleration of this erosion carries real consequences.
escalation_thresholds: China's support-vs-retreat calculus turns on several identifiable thresholds. China increases support when: (1) US actions provide narrative ammunition for multipolarity and Western overreach (the 2026 strikes, conducted one day after a diplomatic breakthrough, fit this pattern); (2) Iranian actions create energy security benefits for China (discounted oil, diversified supply); (3) the conflict weakens US force posture in the Indo-Pacific (THAAD removal, carrier redeployment); (4) diplomatic engagement positions China as a responsible alternative to US unilateralism (envoy tours, UNSC blocking). China quietly steps back when: (1) Iranian actions directly threaten Chinese energy security — the Strait of Hormuz closure endangers Qatari LNG and broader Gulf shipping critical to China, and China is actively pressuring Iran on this; (2) US enforcement actions target core Chinese financial institutions rather than peripheral teapot refineries and shell companies (the current regime targets the periphery, which China can absorb; targeting major state banks or CIPS would be qualitatively different); (3) Iranian escalation risks dragging China into direct confrontation with the US (the nuclear breakout scenario, if it triggers US military strikes on Chinese-supplied systems, would be extremely dangerous for Beijing); (4) the conflict's economic fallout damages Chinese growth prospects — Capital Economics projects China falling below 3% GDP growth in a prolonged war scenario. The fundamental inflection point is when the costs of association with Iran exceed the benefits of cheap oil and geopolitical positioning. Currently, China is on the 'support' side of this ledger — but if Iran's Hormuz closure becomes permanent, if US sanctions shift from peripheral to core Chinese entities, or if the conflict escalates to nuclear dimensions, Beijing's calculus could shift rapidly toward distance. The key asymmetry: China can survive without Iranian oil (it has 1.2–1.4 billion barrels in strategic reserves, roughly 120 days of net imports, plus access to Russian and other suppliers). Iran cannot survive without the Chinese market.

documented_instances.what_happened: Chinese Foreign Minister Wang Yi and Iranian Foreign Minister Zarif signed the 25-year cooperation agreement in Tehran, nominally committing up to $400 billion in Chinese investment in Iran's oil, gas, petrochemical, transportation, and manufacturing sectors in exchange for heavily discounted oil supply.
documented_instances.significance: Established the formal framework for the relationship, though actual implementation has been minimal. China's MFA spokesperson described it as containing 'neither quantitative, specific contracts and goals nor targets any third party' — essentially a framework document rather than binding commitment. Iranian lawmakers raised concerns about parliamentary approval requirements that were never addressed.
documented_instances.source: New York Times (leaked draft, 2020), Petroleum Economist (September 2019 original reporting by Simon Watkins), Wikipedia compilation of verified reporting

documented_instances.event: China grants Iran full BeiDou-3 satellite navigation access
documented_instances.what_happened: China provided Iran with full military access to its BeiDou-3 encrypted satellite navigation system, the Chinese alternative to GPS.
documented_instances.significance: The most significant confirmed military-technology transfer between the two countries. Experts believe Iran is actively using BeiDou-3 to guide drone and missile attacks during the 2026 conflict. This represents a qualitative upgrade to Iranian precision-strike capability that predates the current war.
documented_instances.source: Multiple defense analysts cited in project knowledge assessment; confirmed in ISW/AEI and verified status reports

documented_instances.event: Iran admitted as full SCO member
documented_instances.what_happened: After 15 years of applications, Iran was approved for full Shanghai Cooperation Organization membership at the Dushanbe summit, with Chinese support. Iran became a full member in 2023.
documented_instances.significance: Provided Iran with institutional anchoring in the China-Russia-led multilateral architecture. Iran's President Raisi framed it as proof that 'hegemony and unilateralism are failing.' The SCO now has 10 full members including India, Pakistan, Iran, and Belarus.
documented_instances.source: Iran International, Caspian News, AGSI analysis

documented_instances.what_happened: Iran became a full BRICS member alongside Saudi Arabia, UAE, Egypt, and Ethiopia, with Chinese backing.
documented_instances.significance: Further integrated Iran into the non-Western institutional architecture. However, BRICS' limited cohesion was exposed during the 2026 conflict when it issued a statement expressing 'concern' but took no position on the US-Israeli strikes.
documented_instances.source: Iran International, Gateway Pundit analysis citing BRICS communiqué

documented_instances.event: Chinese oil imports from Iran plummet under US enforcement pressure
documented_instances.what_happened: Under stepped-up Biden/early Trump administration enforcement, Chinese imports of Iranian oil fell from approximately 1.6 million bpd in September 2024 to 740,000 bpd by April 2025 — a halving of volumes — before recovering later in the year.
documented_instances.significance: Demonstrated that US enforcement can meaningfully impact the trade when aggressively pursued, but also that China maintains the infrastructure to rapidly resume purchases when pressure eases. Volumes recovered to 1.38 million bpd average for 2025, showing the resilience of the shadow fleet system.
documented_instances.source: Kpler data cited by Reuters, AGSI analysis (July 2025)

documented_instances.event: UN snapback sanctions reimposed on Iran
documented_instances.what_happened: France, Germany, and the UK invoked the JCPOA snapback mechanism, reimposing UN sanctions on Iran. China opposed the snapback and subsequently blocked UNSC enforcement.
documented_instances.significance: China and Russia's refusal to recognize the 1737 Sanctions Committee's authority rendered multilateral enforcement 'functionally inoperable,' leaving unilateral US action as the primary constraint on the Iran-China oil trade.
documented_instances.source: Gateway Pundit analysis, Iran International, AGSI

documented_instances.what_happened: WSJ reported that Chinese state-owned enterprise Sinosure may have secretly facilitated up to $8.4 billion in investment in Iran through a construction-for-oil exchange, with Chinese companies building airports, refineries, and transport networks in Iran while Iran ships crude to China.
documented_instances.significance: If accurate, this represents the largest confirmed implementation of the 25-year agreement's investment provisions. However, the arrangement uses intermediary structures (Chinese and Hong Kong front companies) to maintain deniability, underscoring that even investment flows operate through sanctions-evasion channels rather than transparent commercial relationships.
documented_instances.source: Wall Street Journal (October 2025), cited by Kharon and Gateway Pundit

documented_instances.event: Pezeshkian-Xi summit at SCO in Tianjin
documented_instances.what_happened: Iranian President Pezeshkian met Xi Jinping in Beijing, pledging to elevate bilateral ties to the 'highest level' and fully implement the 25-year strategic partnership. Pezeshkian called Iran a 'strong and determined' partner, expressed interest in joint high-speed railway and highway projects. Xi called for 'faster implementation' of agreements and described Iran as a 'strategic partner.'
documented_instances.significance: The highest-level reaffirmation of the relationship before the 2026 conflict. Xi's call for 'faster implementation' implicitly acknowledged the gap between the agreement's ambitions and actual progress. Pezeshkian's emphasis on de-dollarization at both BRICS (2024) and SCO (2025) aligned with but did not produce concrete Chinese commitments.
documented_instances.source: Iran International, Caspian News, Iran News Daily, GlobalSecurity.org

documented_instances.event: BRI investment bypasses Iran despite 25-year agreement
documented_instances.what_happened: Green Finance & Development Center data showed Chinese BRI investment surged 62% globally to over $85 billion in 2025, but Iran received almost none. Kazakhstan alone received $25.8 billion. China's official exports to Iran dropped 22% to below $7 billion; non-oil imports fell 32% to $3 billion. Meanwhile, China-Israel trade reached $33 billion with 48% growth.
documented_instances.significance: The starkest evidence that the 25-year agreement remains largely aspirational. Despite rhetorical commitments, Chinese companies and investment institutions are channeling capital to lower-risk destinations. The Iran relationship is overwhelmingly a discounted-oil-for-diplomatic-cover arrangement, not a comprehensive economic partnership. The irony of China-Israel trade exceeding China-Iran non-oil trade is especially notable given the geopolitical alignment claims.
documented_instances.source: Green Finance & Development Center report, Middle East Forum analysis (February 2026)

documented_instances.event: Pre-war stockpiling and February 2026 export surge
documented_instances.what_happened: Iran exported 2.16 million barrels per day in February 2026 — its highest level since July 2018 — virtually all to China. UANI's February 2026 Tanker Tracker confirmed Iran's illicit oil trade was near elevated late-2025 levels just as Operation Epic Fury began.
documented_instances.significance: Suggests coordinated preparation: either Iran was maximizing revenue ahead of anticipated conflict, or Chinese buyers were building buffer stocks. China holds an estimated 1.2–1.4 billion barrels in strategic reserves (roughly 120 days of net imports) plus 40 million barrels in floating storage — positioning it to absorb supply disruption.
documented_instances.source: Project knowledge verified assessment, UANI February 2026 Tanker Tracker

documented_instances.event: China's wartime posture: support with limits
documented_instances.what_happened: China condemned US-Israeli strikes, Wang Yi called Khamenei's killing 'unacceptable,' dispatched Special Envoy Zhai Jun, blocked UNSC enforcement, continued purchasing approximately 1.25 million bpd via shadow fleet with Chinese-flagged vessels granted Strait passage. Simultaneously, China de-escalated around Taiwan (13 days without warplane approaches), pressured Iran on Strait closure to protect Qatari LNG, refused Trump's demand to police the Strait, and provided no confirmed large-scale military resupply.
documented_instances.significance: The clearest real-world demonstration of China's calibrated approach: maximum diplomatic and economic support short of actions that would make it a co-belligerent or provoke direct US confrontation. The Taiwan de-escalation reveals China's priority hierarchy — preserving the Trump-Xi summit and long-term Taiwan preparations outweighs any benefit from exploiting US distraction in the Middle East.
documented_instances.source: Project knowledge verified assessment compiling CNN, SCMP, ISW/AEI, PLATracker, multiple intelligence sources
